quickInsight: When you feel trapped between impossible odds and no way forward, God can still make a path — you just have to keep walking.
keyThemes: Trusting God when the situation looks impossible (Red Sea moment)
God provides what you need, when you need it — not always in advance (manna daily)
You can't do hard things alone — everyone needs an Aaron and Hur holding them up
Complaining vs. trusting: how your attitude shapes your experience
Moving forward even when you can't see the whole path
discussionQuestions: The Israelites were literally trapped — ocean in front, army behind. When have you felt stuck between two bad options? What did you do?
God sent manna every day but told them not to hoard it. Why do you think He didn't just give them a month's supply? What does that teach about how God helps us?
In Exodus 17, Moses couldn't keep his arms up alone — Aaron and Hur had to hold them. Who are the people holding you up right now? Who are you holding up?
The Israelites had just watched God split an ocean, and a few days later they were complaining about water. Why is it so easy to forget what God has done for us? How do you keep from forgetting?
God told Moses to walk toward the sea BEFORE it parted. What's something in your life right now where you might need to take a step forward before you can see how it'll work out?
teachingIdeas: Object lesson — The trust fall: Have a young man stand with his back to the group and fall backward. Connect it to Exodus 14:13-14 — 'Stand still and see the salvation of the Lord.' Sometimes faith means stepping into the unknown and trusting someone has your back.

The manna experiment: Give each young man a handful of small candies. Tell half of them they can save theirs for later, and give the other half the rule that they have to eat them now but they'll get more next week. Discuss what it feels like to trust that more is coming vs. wanting to stockpile. Connect to how God asks us to rely on Him daily — prayers, scripture, sacrament — not just bank up spiritual experiences.

Arms up challenge: Have a young man hold his arms straight out to the sides and time how long he can last (usually about 2 minutes). Then have two friends hold his arms up. Read Exodus 17:12 and talk about how we literally cannot win our battles alone — priesthood quorums, friends, family, and leaders exist for this reason.

Phone parallel: Pull up a phone battery icon. 'Your phone doesn't charge once and last forever — you plug it in every night.' Manna worked the same way. So does your testimony. You can't coast on last year's spiritual experience. What are you doing daily to recharge?
activities: Trust walk obstacle course: Set up an outdoor obstacle course at mutual. Blindfold each young man and have a partner guide him through using only voice directions. Debrief afterward — what was hard about trusting? When did you want to peek? Connect to following God's guidance even when you can't see the full picture.

Hold up your brother challenge: Pair up for a physical activity (wall sits, plank holds, or carrying something heavy together). The rule: if one person quits, both are done. Afterward, discuss what it felt like to keep going because someone was counting on you — and connect to Aaron and Hur holding up Moses' arms in Exodus 17.

Write a 'Red Sea moment' on a card — a time when something seemed impossible but worked out, or something you're facing now that feels impossible. Collect them anonymously and read a few aloud. As a quorum, commit to praying for each other's Red Sea moments this week.
